s1_b1: Lina is hanging a mobile over her little brother's bed. A bar, a string at the middle, shapes dangling off each side.
s1_b2: One goal. It hangs level. Not nose-down on one side.
s1_b3: She hangs the shapes. The bar swings hard to the right and stays there.
s1_b4: So she moves a heavy shape to the left. Now it slams over the other way.
s1_b5: She adds a small shape near the string. It barely changes anything. She doesn't see why.
s1_b6: Heavy near the middle does almost nothing. Light far out swings it a lot. Same weights, opposite effects.
s1_b7: She wasn't short of shapes. She was short of the thing that decides tilt — not weight, but weight times its reach.
s1_b8: Every shape twists the bar by its weight times its distance from the string. That twist is what has to balance.
s1_b9: Level means the left twists and the right twists cancel. Exactly. The total turning effect is zero.
s1_b10: It is the same rule as a long wrench: reach matters as much as force. And it is where the whole thing balances — its center of mass — sitting right under the string.
s1_b11: Before she re-hangs another shape — she builds it where she can see the twist.
s1_b12: Balancing Act. She places the masses at marked distances. Turns the level indicator on.
s1_b13: Here is the real job. Not letting go of the plank. Predicting — before she does.
s1_b14: Where must the last mass go so the left and right twists match — and the plank stays level when she lets go?
s1_b15: Then she lets go. And she watches the level indicator, not the bricks.
s1_b16: After — she explains the gap. Tipped right? The right twist won. By how much tells her how far she was off.
s1_b17: She moves one mass. Predicts again. Three tries. That's all she gets.
s1_b18: Her first prediction is wrong. That is not the problem. That is the point. Each miss tells her which reach she misjudged.
s1_b19: Now she hangs the real mobile. Once. And it floats level over the bed.
s1_b20: The level mobile was never the homework. This is: in one sentence, your own words — why did moving a light shape outward beat moving a heavy one inward?
s1_b21: And if this felt familiar — it should. Do you remember the stuck bolt, and how a longer wrench turned it with a gentler push — reach against force?
s1_b22: And the tumbling wrench whose one quiet point traced a clean arc — the place a thing balances, whether or not it's in the middle?
s1_b23: This rig was both at once — twist, and the point it all balances on. That is the concept.
s1_b24: Now you know which videos to go back to.
